ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for 'spicy boiled pork slices hot pot' specifically; mapped to broader 'pork dish' class (FOODON_03302757) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for a spiced, boiled pork preparation; no specific hot-pot pork dish code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for 'shui zhu rou pian' or Chinese spicy boiled pork hot pot; A0CKP (cooked pork) used as the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to spicy boiled pork slices hot pot; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Spicy Boiled Pork Slices Hot Pot is approximately 450 g, comprising the pork and broth component together with one portion of steamed white rice as served in a typical Singapore hawker or restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: Spicy Boiled Pork Slices Hot Pot 水煮肉片小火锅 — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database (G00010499)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo record G00010499 at confidence 0.95. The HPB record notes that the nutrition data reflects the pork component of the dish; the included rice portion is listed in the dish name but its macronutrients are not fully integrated into the per-100g figures, which explains the 0 g carbohydrate reading. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central pork belly / shoulder cooked analogues (NDB 10006 / 10219) as approximately 3.5 g/100g. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are set to -1 as HPB does not report them for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed pork-and-rice hot pot dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB per-100g data for this record reflects the pork component only (carbohydrate 0 g/100g), which would yield a GI of not-applicable for the pork fraction alone. However, the dish name explicitly states it includes one portion of steamed white rice. Steamed white rice has a well-established GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283). The GI of 72 is assigned to the composite dish on the basis of the rice component, which is the dominant glycaemic driver. Glycemic load per serving is calculated on the pork-only per-100g carbohydrate figure (0 g) and is therefore 0.0 for the pork fraction; the rice portion GL is not integrated because the rice macronutrients are not included in the HPB per-100g record. This limitation is documented in notes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — critically elevated, far exceeding the 600 mg/100g clinical threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this dish; a single 450 g serving delivers approximately 4725 mg sodium, nearly double the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg, and will acutely raise blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Included rice portion adds significant glycaemic load despite pork component having negligible carbohydrate; high sodium also worsens insulin resistance indirectly
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller rice portion or substitute with cauliflower rice, monitor total carbohydrate intake from the rice component, and be aware that the high sodium content (1050 mg/100g) may worsen cardiovascular comorbidities common in diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g combined with 10.5 g/100g total fat and 89 mg/100g cholesterol creates a high cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish due to critically high sodium (1050 mg/100g) which promotes fluid retention and hypertension, combined with saturated fat and dietary cholesterol that contribute to atherogenic risk; if consumed, portion size must be strictly limited and sauce intake minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplifying inflammatory pathways; sodium 1050 mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue oedema; chili (nightshade) may trigger joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; the combination of red meat arachidonic acid load, critically high sodium, and nightshade chili ingredients collectively amplifies systemic inflammation and joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Boiled Pork Slices Hot Pot (水煮肉片小火锅) is a Sichuan-origin Chinese dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants. It consists of thinly sliced pork (typically pork belly or shoulder) that is briefly poached in a richly spiced broth made from doubanjiang (spicy fermented bean paste), chili oil, garlic, ginger, Sichuan peppercorn, and soy sauce. The dish is served in a small individual hot pot vessel and is accompanied by one portion of steamed white rice. The flavour profile is intensely spicy, savoury, and numbing (mala), with a silky, tender pork texture and a deeply aromatic chili-oil broth. Per 100 g of the pork component, the dish provides 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, and 0 g carbohydrate (the HPB record reflects the pork fraction only; the rice portion contributes additional carbohydrate not captured in the per-100g figure). Sodium is critically high at 1050 mg/100g, making this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The included rice portion raises the overall glycaemic load of the meal significantly. Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the rice component. It is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to red meat arachidonic acid content, high sodium, and nightshade chili. The dish is not halal as it contains pork.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo record G00010499 at confidence 0.95 (canonical, reviewed). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central cooked pork analogues and documented. GI assigned by analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (steamed white rice, GI 72) due to the included rice component. Ontology mapped to defensible broader anchors with gap notes.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is 0.0 and sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Foodo G00010499; the HPB per-100g record reflects the pork component of the dish only. The dish name states rice is included, but the rice macronutrients are not integrated into the HPB per-100g figures. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB for the pork component; the vegetable garnish contribution is negligible and not separately quantified in the source record. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES (-1): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1 — HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid fractions for this record; USDA FoodData Central cooked pork entries do not provide a direct match sufficient to confidently assign these values without risk of misrepresentation. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1 — not reported by HPB Foodo for this record; detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific prepared dish are not available in the HPB database as of 2026. 3. SATURATED FAT: saturated_fat_g estimated at 3.5 g/100g from USDA FoodData Central cooked pork shoulder/belly analogues (SR Legacy NDB 10006, 10219) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this record; this is an adapted estimate. 4. GI LIMITATION: The glycemic_load_per_serving of 0.0 reflects only the pork component carbohydrate (0 g/100g × 450 g / 100 = 0 g available carbohydrate from pork). The rice portion GL is not calculable from the HPB per-100g record alone. The composite meal GL is substantially higher due to the rice; consumers and clinicians should account for the rice portion separately. 5. HALAL STATUS: This dish contains pork and is not halal. 6.
